title: Chapter 18: Cold Eternity
paragraphs: Night at the walls of Etcha descended like a heavy, frozen shroud. Over the last few years, the 'Grey City' of refugees had become a permanent scar on the body of the earth. Ash continued to fall, covering thousands of temporary shacks. The four outcasts settled in their usual niche between the buttresses, which had become their home over this time.

Yashil sat by the small campfire. Today he was especially exhausted: he had spent the entire day in the lower quarters of the camp, where bone fever had broken out. He had given away the last drops of healing tinctures to children, barely able to stand on his feet from fatigue. His face, sunken and pale, glowed with that quiet determination that seven years had failed to break.
Rene huddled in her cloak. The tip of her minty cigarette was the only bright spot in the gray dusk.

"Five years we've been rotting under these walls, Yashil," the fox said melancholically. "Five years you've been dividing one flatbread among ten people and treating those who will die tomorrow anyway. You are the most stubborn idiot I've known in both my centuries."
"A wolf doesn't count days, little fox. He counts scars. Our green brother has gained many over these years, but not a single one has made him angrier. Auf. It scares even me."
Kirah sat a little apart. He looked into the darkness. His veins glowed dimly.
"Something is out there," the giant rumbled. "Darkness, but... not like Zahan's."
The campfire was instantly extinguished. Step sounds came from the impenetrable gloom. It smelled of old blood and withered roses. In the pale light of the runes, Lord Valthor appeared.
The vampire stopped a few paces from Yashil.

"Five years," Valthor said in his insinuating voice. "Five years I have been watching your ridiculous experiment from my catacombs, goblin. I saw how you froze but gave your blanket to a dwarven orphan. I saw how you stood between gangs of marauders, armed only with your faith in their remnants of conscience. And what's most amazing... you're still alive."

"The High Elves for centuries built pride into an absolute. But you, sitting in this dirt for five years, have created more order and mercy here than they have in their White Halls. Tell me, Yashil of the Rotten Hollow... do you really believe you can outweigh the scales of this entire rotting universe with your tiny good deeds?"
Yashil calmly met the gaze of the immortal predator.

"I'm not trying to outweigh the scales, Lord Valthor," he replied quietly. "I just don't want my side of the scale to be empty. The world is dying. But as long as we share this fire and this water, we are not just dust on the wind."
Valthor was silent for a long time. Not a single muscle moved on his sculpted face.

"Compassion is a crack in the armor," the vampire said coldly. "But I admit: your crack has proven stronger than elven jade. You call me a dead man. Perhaps. But you are the first living person whose death I will be bored to watch."
The vampire began to dissolve in the frosty mist.
"Soon Zahan will strike again. And it won't be just a raid. It will be the finale. Keep your fire until then, goblin. I will come to look at it one last time."
